groups.name: Sickle Cell Anemia
groups.explanation: A point mutation (Glu→Val at position 6 of β-globin) makes hemoglobin polymerize when deoxygenated — the cells sickle, clog, and hemolyze.

article.text: A single point mutation swaps glutamate for valine at position 6 of the β-globin chain. Under low oxygen tension, the abnormal hemoglobin (HbS) polymerizes into rigid fibers that distort the red cell into its namesake sickle shape.

Sickled cells clog small vessels — vaso-occlusion — causing pain crises and, in infants and toddlers, dactylitis (painful swelling of the hands and feet from small-bone marrow infarction). The same cells are fragile and hemolyze early, and repeated splenic infarcts eventually destroy the spleen (autosplenectomy), leaving Howell-Jolly bodies on the smear and a lasting vulnerability to encapsulated organisms.
Hydroxyurea treats the disease upstream: it raises fetal hemoglobin (HbF), which doesn't sickle and dilutes the HbS inside each cell, cutting the frequency of pain crises.

groups.name: Graves Disease
groups.explanation: An IgG autoantibody (TSI) mimics TSH, driving the whole thyroid to overproduce hormone.

article.text: Graves disease is driven by an IgG autoantibody, thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI), that binds and activates the TSH receptor itself. The thyroid reads that as a maximal "make more hormone" signal, so free T4 climbs while pituitary TSH is suppressed by feedback.

Because TSI acts on the whole gland at once, radioactive iodine uptake is diffuse and homogeneous — not the single "hot" nodule of a toxic adenoma. Exophthalmos and pretibial myxedema come from the same antibody-driven process, as retro-orbital and skin fibroblasts pile up glycosaminoglycans; both are essentially unique to Graves among the causes of hyperthyroidism.
Methimazole blocks thyroid peroxidase to slow hormone synthesis; propylthiouracil (PTU) takes over specifically in the first trimester of pregnancy and in thyroid storm.

groups.name: Infective Endocarditis
groups.explanation: Bacteria seed a heart valve, building fibrin-platelet vegetations that shower emboli to fingers, toes, and beyond.

article.text: Bacteria in the bloodstream settle on a heart valve and recruit platelets and fibrin, building a friable vegetation that mixes organisms, fibrin, and neutrophils.

Pieces of that vegetation break off and shower emboli to the periphery: tender Osler nodes on the finger and toe pads, painless Janeway lesions on the palms and soles, splinter hemorrhages under the nails, and Roth spots in the retina.

The organism sets the tempo. Viridans streptococci cause a slower, subacute course, classically after a dental procedure seeds a valve already damaged by prior disease; Staphylococcus aureus causes an acute, more destructive infection, often the tricuspid valve in IV drug use. Diagnosis leans on the modified Duke criteria — blood cultures plus echocardiographic evidence of a vegetation.

groups.name: Wilson Disease
groups.explanation: A defective copper-transport pump (ATP7B) lets copper build up in liver, brain, and eye.

article.text: Wilson disease is a defect in ATP7B, the copper-transporting pump that normally loads copper into bile for excretion. Without it, copper backs up — first in the liver, causing hepatitis and cirrhosis at a young age, then spilling over into the basal ganglia and the eye.

The classic triad is liver disease, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and the Kayser-Fleischer ring — copper deposited in Descemet's membrane at the corneal limbus. On liver biopsy, a rhodanine stain lights the copper up directly, as orange-red granules inside hepatocytes.

The labs are a common trap: ceruloplasmin is LOW (it's the copper-carrier protein Wilson disease depletes) while urinary copper excretion is HIGH (free copper spilling out through the kidneys). Treatment chelates the excess copper — penicillamine or trientine, both copper CHELATORS, not antibiotics — plus oral zinc, which blocks further copper absorption in the gut.
